questions.question: Define a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) and explain why topological sorting is only possible for DAGs.
keyPoints: DAG is a directed graph with no directed cycles
Following edges never returns to starting vertex
Topological sort requires a linear order respecting all directed edges
If a cycle exists, no linear ordering can satisfy all edge constraints
Cycle makes dependencies unsatisfiable

questions.question: Explain Kahn's Algorithm for topological sorting with its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Calculate in-degree for all vertices
Initialize queue with all zero in-degree vertices
Process queue: remove vertex, add to result, reduce neighbor in-degrees
Add new zero in-degree vertices to queue
Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V)

questions.question: How does DFS-based topological sort work? What data structure is used to obtain the final order?
keyPoints: Perform DFS on the graph
Explore all neighbors of a vertex before adding it to result
Use stack to store vertices after complete exploration
Pop from stack to get topological order in reverse finish time
Time complexity: O(V+E)

questions.question: How can topological sorting be used to detect cycles in a directed graph?
keyPoints: In Kahn's algorithm, if queue becomes empty before processing all vertices, cycle exists
Remaining vertices have non-zero in-degrees forming a cycle
If processed vertices count < total vertices, cycle is present
DFS method detects back edges to vertices in recursion stack

questions.question: What are source and sink vertices in a DAG? How are they identified?
keyPoints: Source vertex: in-degree = 0 (no incoming edges)
Sink vertex: out-degree = 0 (no outgoing edges)
Source has no prerequisites, can start processing first
Sink represents final task with no dependents
Identified by scanning adjacency list/in-degree array

questions.question: Give at least three real-world applications of DAGs and topological ordering.
keyPoints: Task scheduling and project management
Course prerequisite planning in universities
Build systems (Makefiles) for compiling code
Package dependency resolution (npm, pip)
Instruction scheduling in compilers
Deadlock detection in operating systems

questions.question: What determines whether a topological ordering is unique? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Topological order is unique only when DAG forms a linear chain
If multiple source vertices exist at any point, different orders possible
Example: A→B, A→C - B and C can be ordered either way
Linear chain A→B→C→D has exactly one valid ordering
Non-unique when parallel edges exist in dependency graph

questions.question: Consider the graph: A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D, B→E, C→E. Trace through Kahn's Algorithm step by step showing the queue contents and the final topological order obtained.
keyPoints: Calculate in-degrees: A=0, B=1, C=1, D=2, E=2
Initialize queue with A (in-degree 0)
Process A: remove, reduce B,C to 0, add to queue
Process B: remove, reduce D,E to 1
Process C: remove, reduce D,E to 0, add to queue
Process D: remove, E stays 0
Process E: remove
Final order: A, B, C, D, E
Alternative valid order: A, C, B, D, E

questions.question: Write pseudocode for both Kahn's Algorithm and DFS-based topological sorting. Compare their approaches and state when each is preferable.
keyPoints: Kahn's: Queue-based BFS approach using in-degrees
DFS: Stack-based using finish times
Kahn's preferred for cycle detection and when vertex processing order matters
DFS preferred when recursion stack available and simpler implementation needed
Both O(V+E) time, O(V) space
Kahn's better for parallel processing scenarios

questions.question: A software company needs to develop a build system. The build process has the following dependencies: core must be built before util, util before io, io before ui, core before math, math before app, app before product. Also ui and product must be built before release. Design a DAG to represent these dependencies and determine a valid build order using topological sort.
keyPoints: Vertices: core, util, io, ui, math, app, product, release
Edges: core→util, util→io, io→ui, core→math, math→app, app→product, ui→release, product→release
In-degrees: core=0, util=1, io=1, ui=1, math=1, app=1, product=1, release=2
Queue starts: core
Order: core → util → io → math → ui → app → product → release
All dependencies satisfied in this order
This is one valid topological ordering

questions.question: Prove that a directed graph has a topological ordering if and only if it is acyclic (i.e., is a DAG).
keyPoints: If part: For DAG, Kahn's algorithm or DFS produces topological order
Every DAG has at least one source vertex (in-degree 0)
Remove source, repeat - process yields valid ordering
Only-if part: If graph has topological order, assume cycle exists
In cycle, no vertex can come first - contradiction
Therefore, topological order implies acyclic nature
